Award Title: Enhanced Heat Flux Closure from Data Driven Modeling
RI Name: Virginia Polytechnic Institute and State University
Research Area Keywords: AI/ML,  Computational Fluid Dynamics,  Hypersonics,  Neural networks,  Turbulence, Hypersonics
Abstract: Accurate prediction of surface heat flux in turbulent flow fields is critical for the design of air breathing hypersonic flight vehicles, to inform efficient thermal protection systems, and mitigate in-flight deformations leading to scramjet engine unstart. Current workhorse modeling methods, such as Reynolds Averaged Navier-Stokes (RANS) with conventional turbulence closure models, cannot accurately characterize hypersonic flowfield features, especially near regions with strong shocks, shockwave-boundary layer interactions, and other flow phenomenology encountered during hypersonic flight. Recent developments in the field of artificial intelligence and machine learning (AI/ML) enable data-driven and physics-informed deep neural networks to model the turbulent heat transport closure, provide accurate predictions of surface quantities, and enhance computational design efforts for hypersonic flight vehicles. In this STTR effort, Spectral Sciences, Inc. and Virginia Tech will extend and apply their novel AI/ML training framework for enhanced, model-agnostic turbulent heat flux closure for Navy-relevant hypersonic air breathing vehicles.

Award Title: Development and Demonstration of an Efficient Multiphysics Modeling Framework for Rain-Induced Damage and Aerodynamic Effects on Hypersonic Vehicles
Research Area Keywords: computational fluid dynamics,  droplet impingement,  material response,  multiphase flows,  multiphysics simulation,  shock-induced aerobreakup,  weather erosion, Hypersonic vehicles
Abstract: Spectral Sciences, Inc. proposes to develop a framework for linking high-fidelity multiphase flow simulations and peridynamic solid simulations through automated mapping of results between the models. This effort will include the incorporation of a novel multiphase numerical method, capable of simulating shock induced aerobreakup, and a material response algorithm for modeling the deformation and fracture of vehicle surfaces for the purpose of simulating rain-induced damage on hypersonic flight vehicles. This effort will leverage SSIís extensive experience with aerothermal modeling of hypersonic vehicles with US3D with extensions. Phase I will develop and demonstrate the coupling between the multiphase extension of US3D and the peridynamic solver for the deformable vehicle surface. Phase II will develop the full prototype mapping between the two simulations and improve the efficiency of the simulation framework with multiresolution mesh capabilities. This effort will improve the simulation capabilities for modeling rain-induced damage on realistic hypersonic vehicle geometries.ÝÝ

Award Title: Standardized All-Altitude Modulation Model (SAMM) for Long-wave Infrared through Mid-wave Ultraviolet Environmental Effects Generation
Research Area Keywords: EO/IR,  non-LTE,  SAMM,  Ultraviolet, Background
Abstract: The challenge of detecting and tracking hypersonic vehicles by missile defense sensor systems may require observations at non-traditional wavelengths and at non-nadir viewing geometries.  Relatively dim signals may be obscured by the naturally occurring background environment, including atmospheric attenuation, non-local thermodynamic equilibrium (NLTE) earth limb radiation, aurora, and cloud background effects.  Further complications arise when utilizing off-nadir and limb viewing geometries resulting in significantly greater impacts from refraction, non-uniform ground surface distances on the focal plane, and the significant increase in dynamic range due to the Earth background. This Direct-to-Phase-II proposes to address known gaps in existing environmental background modeling capabilities.  The solution will be integrated into the Standardized All-Altitude Modulation Model (SAMM) and the Standardized Atmosphere Generator (SAG), preserving and enhancing the decades-long investment by the Missile Defense Agency (MDA) into the creation of these two software tools.  This Direct-to-Phase-II further proposes to develop these capabilities as an integrable module for other MDA capabilities, including the Fast Line-of-sight Imagery for Target and Exhaust Signatures (FLITES) scene generator for Hardware in the Loop (HWIL) and the Objective Simulation Framework (OSF) higher level architecture.

Award Title: Zernike Polynomials via Phase Recovery
Research Area Keywords: calibration,  optical path difference,  phase recovery,  wavefront error,  Zernike, Optical Aberration
Abstract: The Army is working to incorporate identifying and tracking targets, adjusting aim for ballistics, and compensating for wind into the next generation squad weapon. Fire control devices to identify and track movement are being developed for targeting and to steady aim. The Army is developing rifle integrated optics providing shooters with enhanced viewing to increase hit probability and reduce time to engagement and increase target recognition. To successfully develop such advanced fire control systems, the Army needs to fully characterize the wavefront error of optical systems. Commercial systems are based on interferometric methods and are unable to extract off-axis aberrations, like coma. The Army requires a non-interferometric method comprised of only a collimated, partially-coherent light source, a microscope objective, and a sensing array. Spectral Sciences, Inc. (SSI) and the University of Rochester (UR) propose to develop the Phase Recovery Imaging Microscope for Aberration (PRIMA), a prototype hardware and software system that provides the focus scan measurements necessary to find the complex Zernike coefficients by iteratively evaluating the Extended Nijboer-Zernike (ENZ) formulas. The design specification will be determined using a novel combination of hardware developed by UR with an Algorithm Development Kit (ADK) developed at SSI. The ADK will provide a unified software interface for calibration, algorithm testing, and performance evaluation. The Phase I proof-of-concept will be a demonstration of the technique on a lens with known aberrations. The Phase II product will be the hardware and software necessary to realize our procedure on a commercial system.

Award Title: Data-Driven Predictive Sporadic-E Model (D2PSEM)
Research Area Keywords: ionosonde,  ionospheric model,  radio occultation, Sporadic E
Abstract: Civilian and military communications, navigation, and radar applications depend on predictable propagation of radio waves through the ionosphere. These applications are severely impacted by irregular ionospheric structures that can suddenly alter the propagation properties, including scintillation of transmitted waves. Spectral Sciences, Inc. proposes to develop a forecasting capability of ionospheric irregularities. The Data-Driven Predictive Sporadic E Model (D2PSEM) will be an algorithm which provides a two-week to one-month forecast of the time-dependent probability of occurrence of sporadic E layer of the ionosphere above a user-defined density threshold at user-specified locations on the globe.D2PSEM will operate on measured data to establish a set of correlations between relevant physical quantities in the temporal and spatial domains. Once developed, this function will be used to identify the key physical quantities of interest from a known set of correlative properties. The comprehensive correlation model will then be the kernel of a Data Assimilation Predictive Model that propagates occurrences while updating the correlation coefficients based on the latest ionospheric data.

Award Title: Enhancement and Validation of Fluid-Thermal-Structural Interaction Algorithms for Hypersonic Air-Breathing Systems
Research Area Keywords: aerothermoelasticity,  Computational Fluid Dynamics,  Conjugate Heat Transfer,  Finite Element Analysis,  Fluid-thermal-structural interactions,  hypersonic,  Thermostructural, aerothermoelasticity
Abstract: Spectral Sciences, Inc., in collaboration with Texas A&M University, proposes to enhance a framework that couples high-fidelity, aerothermal gas flow simulations and nonlinear thermomechanical finite element analysis (FEA) to analyze hypersonic air-breathing systems.  Novel data, which focuses on the fluid-thermal-structural (FTS) interactions for complex, 3-Dimensional shapes, will be measured and used as validation for the existing and enhanced algorithms. This effort will leverage SSI’s extensive experience with aerothermal modeling of hypersonic vehicles within industry-standard simulation tools, leveraging extensions through a plugin architecture.  Phase I will focus on demonstrating the feasibility of the innovative approach and initiating collection of critical validation data. Phase II will focus on development and validation of the algorithms, leveraging measured data. The capabilities will be integrated within industry-standard simulation suites, transitioning to application across the community.

Award Title: Compact, Attritable, Midwave Hyperspectral Imager (CAM-HSI)
Research Area Keywords: Attritable,  HSI,  Hyperspectral Imager,  Infrared,  MWIR,  UAS, Midwave
Abstract: Spectral Sciences, Inc. (SSI) has developed a design for, and proven feasibility of, a compact, push broom style, midwave infrared (MWIR) hyperspectral imager (HSI) capable of spectral detection and identification of ground targets from an altitude of 6 km (20,000 ft). To meet the high sensitivity requirement for long-range detection, the sensor uses a high-throughput, high-resolution cryo-cooled spectrograph with a low-noise MWIR focal plane array (FPA). The sensor design has diffraction limited spatial and spectral resolution over the primary spectral range of interest of 3-5.1 microns and a noise equivalent spectral radiance (NESR) of 1 microflick sampling at 100 Hz with 3.75 nm spectral channels.   We have developed a roadmap for building and testing a prototype sensor in Phase II to demonstrate the optical performance and detection sensitivity.  This would serve as a first step to a ruggedized Phase III sensor for incorporation in a tactical gimbal that can be used on attritable platforms.  Such a sensor would have tremendous advantages compared to other HSI sensors in that almost all materials have sharp, identifiable features in the MWIR spectral range.

Award Title: High-Altitude, Long-Endurance, Visible Through Extended SWIR Hyperspectral Imaging for Earth Sciences
Abstract: NASA has identified long-endurance, sub-orbital, stratospheric observations from High-Altitude Platform Systems (HAPS) as a key future capability for the Surface Biology and Geology (SBG) mission and supporting the needs of commercial and government organizations for ecological, climate, earth resource and emergency management information.  A key source of data for SBG and other applications is hyperspectral imaging (HSI) over the Visible through Short Wave Infrared spectral region (VSWIR).  VSWIR HSI systems have been flown on aircraft below 50,000 ft and recently on low-Earth orbiting satellites. However, these platforms do not meet the combined wide-area coverage, spatial resolution, station keeping and long duration diurnal measurement requirements of SBG measurement campaigns.   A HAPS VSWIR HSI solution is therefore an important science mission objective for NASA.  Spectral Sciences Inc proposes an innovative combination of our rugged Terrestrial Hyperspectral Imaging Apparatus (THIA) VSWIR HSI and the Sceye Inc. Stratospheric HAPS to meet SBG sensing needs.  In Phase I, SSI developed a concept for the THIA-HAPS payload and preliminary mission plan for a THIA-HAPS demonstration flight.  In Phase II, our team proposes to fly the THIA-HAPS payload, in a 14-day demonstration of HAPS capability to collect VSWIR HSI imagery from 50,000 to 60,000 ft.

Award Title: MCScene-Based Oceanic Radiative Transfer Testbed
Research Area Keywords: Hyperspectral Imaging,  Littoral Zone,  Maritime/Aquatic Remote Sensing,  Polarization,  Radiative Transfer, Radiative Transfer
Abstract: There remains an urgent need within the Department of the Navy (DON) for a radiative transfer (RT) testbed that can accurately synthesize hyperspectral imagery (HSI) to support the design of remote sensing instruments, development of new retrieval algorithms, and quantification of uncertainties. However, remote sensing in the littoral zone requires accurate modeling of signatures in complex 3D environments which may include cloud fields, structured backgrounds, weather, rough sea state conditions, coastal influences, land topography, underwater turbidity, surface bathymetry and benthic cover, and embedded targets. To address the need for a testbed that permits modelling of complex littoral zone/maritime environments, Spectral Sciences, Inc. (SSI) proposes to develop COAST, the Coupled Ocean-Atmospheric Spectral-imagery Testbed, a verified (via code intercomparison) and validated (against HSI measurement data) product that combines maritime enabled extensions of the well-established 1-D (MODTRAN) and 3-D (MCScene) radiative transfer codes developed by SSI. COAST will enable users to simulate scenes easily and accurately with an arbitrary combination of land, shallow ocean, and deep-water elements.

Award Title: Numerical Design Tool for Space-Borne, Plasma-Coupled VLF Antenna Systems
Research Area Keywords: VLF; Radiation Belt Remediation; Magnetosphere; Plasmasphere; Space-Based Antenna; High-Altitude Nuclear Explosion; Particle-in-Cell
Abstract: Relativistic electrons trapped by Earth’s magnetic field pose a threat to spacecraft in low Earth orbit (LEO). High-power, very low frequency (VLF) whistler waves can remediate this threat by deflecting electrons to smaller pitch angles that take them deeper into Earth’s atmosphere. Such VLF transmission was demonstrated with the Demonstration and Science Experiments (DSX) mission. The complex interaction between antenna and local plasma calls for high-fidelity models that allow the computation of the performance of a specific antenna design. To address this gap, SSI proposes to develop a Model for Antennas Immersed in PLasma (MAIPL). MAIPL will consist of a 3D, electromagnetic (EM) particle-in-cell (PIC) solver that calculates antenna surface charging in the presence of a magnetized plasma, the resulting near-field radiation pattern, and the performance of the system in terms of radiated power and efficiency. MAIPL will also characterize the effects of an artificial plasma source on the system. In Phase I, a reduced-dimensionality EM PIC model will be developed and applied to demonstrate propagation of VLF radiation in representative problems. In Phase II, the model will be extended to 3D with multi-scale mesh and parallel computing capabilities that then can be applied to dipole and parametric loop antennas.

Award Title: Feature-aware Ablation and Reaction Simulator for Thermal Extremes
RI Name: University of Colorado Boulder-Office of Contracts and Grants
Research Area Keywords: Ablation,  Carbon,  CFD ,  Finite-Rate,  hypersonic,  Mesoscale,  TPS, Finite-Rate
Abstract: Hypersonic vehicles require thermal protection systems (TPS) for protection from high-temperature, strongly shocked air. Carbon-based ablative TPS have received particular attention for Navy applications. Since ground-based testing at flight-like non-equilibrium conditions is limited and costly, physics-based simulation tools, including comprehensive finite-rate gas-gas and gas-surface chemistry models, are essential for designing next-generation TPS materials. The intricate interplay between gases and materials during hypersonic flight induces a degradation process, leading to alterations in surface morphology, and subsequently influencing the ablation dynamics within the TPS material. Moreover, these morphological/surface transformations have the potential to impact the gas-surface interactions throughout the flight. However, the current finite-rate ablation models for these systems are not tuned to chemical and morphological structure of the TPS material and do not account for mesoscale structural changes that occur during a flight. To address these gaps, Spectral Sciences, Inc. (SSI), in collaboration with the University of Colorado Boulder (CU), the University of Minnesota (UMN) and GoHypersonic, Inc. (GHI), proposes to develop an innovative software suite to model ablation of carbon-based TPS materials on hypersonic vehicles, with finite-rate chemistry and mesoscale material physics in the framework of computational fluid dynamics (CFD). Our team combines expertise in both hypersonic vehicle simulations and state-of-the-art experimental gas-surface reaction dynamics. In the Phase I effort, we demonstrated the feasibility of extensions to the state-of-the-art Air Carbon Ablation (ACA) finite-rate model, informed by new experimental data and integrated into the US3D hypersonic CFD code. In the Phase II program, we will implement advanced models of molecular and mesoscale ablation processes, guided by a comprehensive experimental validation campaign on Navy-relevant materials. The final product will meet critical Government and commercial needs to understand and manage gas–material interactions in extreme thermal environments.

Award Title: Replay Engineering Workflow In Networked Defense Federations (REWIND-F)
Research Area Keywords: Checkpoint,  M&S Federations,  M&S Frameworks,  Simulation,  Simulation Playback,  Simulation Restart,  State Saves, Model
Abstract: Spectral Sciences, Inc. proposes to implement a software federation framework that supports a modular restart and playback capability that can be incorporated into other frameworks such as the Object Simulation Framework. A software federation is a collection of software acting in concert to perform a single simulation. This is sometimes referred to as a “simulation of simulations.” The Missile Defense Agency has a need for the ability to “rewind” a federation simulation to a point in its history and replay the simulation using the same or different execution parameters in the context of a ballistic missile defense system. Use cases include recovering from hardware failures and varying the execution to affect different scenario outcomes. The challenge is that federate models are not necessarily designed for use as part of a software federation. Spectral Sciences, Inc. will extend an existing in-house framework developed for cloud computing and parallel computation pipelines to include support for time-dependent federation execution. Furthermore, a key design goal is a minimally invasive approach to federate participation which includes the restart and playback capability. We will also research the complex topic of operating-system-based checkpoint and restart for possible introduction in the future. Approved for Public Release | 25-MDA-12052 (27 Mar 25)

Award Title: Terminal phase of training Decision Support Tool (TDST)
Research Area Keywords: directed energy,  double descent,  network explainability,  neural collapse,  terminal phase of training, Machine learning
Abstract: AFRL/RD has an interest in the phenomenon of double descent or “neural collapse” (NC) in which a deep learning model’s training loss drops dramatically after an extended period of flattening. If a model training is ended before double decent occurs, as is typical with early stopping, the global minimum will not be reached by the optimization algorithm and a suboptimal solution will result, leading to a model will fit to make accurate inferences (e.g., object classification or segmentation). By extension, since the Air Force requires reliable and accurate deep learning models for directed energy (DE) applications, explaining and predicting neural collapse is important. Spectral Sciences, Inc. (SSI), in collaboration with DE experts at the Air Force Institute of Technology (AFIT), propose to develop a software tool called Terminal phase of training Decision Support Tool” (TDST). TDST works by monitoring multiple layers of the network during training, evaluating the likelihood double decent will occur and providing the model engineer quantitative metrics to consider. In the process, we also plan to interpret how and why neural collapse occurs, contributing to existing research in explainable AI..  Phase I will result in a demonstration showing double descent and quantifying its effect on DE-relevant datasets, and the development of a prototype explainability analysis tool. In Phase II, the software tool will be expanded to include the prediction of double descents that may be possible during training but have not yet been reached. Additionally, the tool will be expanded to characterize and quantify the specific type of collapse expected. The software will be generalized for deployment across different neural network architectures and datasets. Phase III will expand the Technology Readiness Level (TRL) of TDST into a full end-to-end DE simulation framework.

Award Title: Physics-Informed Machine Learning for Hypersonic Turbulence
Research Area Keywords: Artificial Intelligence,  Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD)?,  Deep Learning,  Hypersonics,  Machine Learning,  Neural networks,  Turbulence, Turbulence
Abstract: Turbulence modeling for compressible flow is essential for accurate modeling of heat transfer, skin friction, and pressure surrounding hypersonic vehicles to correctly predict vehicle survivability. High fidelity computational fluid dynamics (CFD) approaches are too computationally demanding and require faster, workhorse methods such as Reynolds Averaged Navier-Stokes (RANS) simulations for computation of flight trajectories and dynamics. However, current RANS turbulence closure models cannot accurately characterize hypersonic flowfield features. Physics-informed machine learning (PIML) methods provide an avenue for creation of more accurate turbulence models. In this STTR effort, Spectral Sciences, Inc. and Virginia Tech will develop an innovative PIML turbulence closure model leveraging recent advances in ensemble learning to enable efficient training with both experimental and high-fidelity simulation data in a unified data-driven framework.

Award Title: Airborne Field Calibration System for LWIR HSI Vapor Quantification
Research Area Keywords: calibration,  counter-WMD,  infrared,  LWIR,  transmittance,  UAS, Hyperspectral
Abstract: Passive Long Wave Infrared (LWIR) Hyperspectral Imagers (HSI) are among the best sensors for quantifying dangerous gas clouds from weapons of mass destruction (WMD), counter-WMD operations, and fugitive emissions from industry and agriculture. LWIR HSI is very sensitive for detecting chemical vapors, but quantification of concentrations can be ambiguous as the observed signals have a complicated dependence on atmospheric conditions and temperatures. Much progress has been made in quantifying concentration using sophisticated retrieval algorithms, but truth data is required for calibration.   We propose an airborne calibration system that removes all ambiguity from concentration measurements using a dual-temperature blackbody source mounted on a Class1 UAS to make a simple and direct absorption measurement of the chemical in the glass cloud. A ground station compiles HSI data from the sensor under test with position and temperature information from the UAS to make transmission measurements of the atmosphere with and without the chemical cloud.  Absolute concentrations determined for the pixel viewing the UAS-mounted source can be compared directly to the retrieved concentrations in neighboring pixels to provide calibration for the sensor under test and its retrieval algorithm.

Award Title: Compact, Attritable, Midwave Hyperspectral Imager (CAM-HSI)
Research Area Keywords: Attritable,  Hyperspectral Imager,  Infrared,  UAS, Midwave
Abstract: Spectral Sciences proposes to meet the need for ISR sensors for use in attritable vehicles with an innovative, compact, midwave infrared hyperspectral imager.  Operational hyperspectral imagers (HSI) operating in the visible-to-SWIR and longwave infrared spectral regions have demonstrated ability for material classification and target identification.  Mid-wave HSI sensors offer both day/night operability and strong sensitivity to gaseous species that are indicative of vehicle traffic and industrial activity.  They require, however extremely high spectral resolution and high throughput to see through the atmosphere, and in most cases require cooled optics, resulting in previous designs that are large and expensive.  SSI proposes an innovative design with minimal cooling requirements, high resolution, low Size, Weight, and Power (SWaP) that can achieve better than 1 microFlick NESR across 500 bands covering 3-5 microns.  The design concept calls for snap-together machined optical components and minimum alignment, which should result in relatively low costs when procured in volumes of tens-to-hundreds.  In Phase I we will develop a detailed design for a demonstrator optical system to be fabricated assembled and tested in Phase II.  Phase III development will lead to a fully functional low-SWAP-C system complete with on-board data processing suitable for flight on an autonomous vehicle.

Award Title: Replay Engineering Workflow In Networked Defense Federations?(REWIND-F)
Research Area Keywords: Checkpoint,  M&S Federations,  M&S Frameworks,  Simulation,  Simulation Playback,  Simulation Restart,  State Saves, Model
Abstract: Spectral Sciences, Inc. proposes to implement a software federation framework that supports restart and playback. A software federation is a collection of software acting in concert to perform a single simulation. This is sometimes referred to as a ōsimulation of simulations.ö The Missile Defense Agency has a need for the ability to ōrewindö a federation simulation to a point in its history and replay the simulation using the same or different input parameters in the context of a ground-based midcourse defense engagement scenario. Use cases include recovering from a hardware failure and performing trade studies. The challenge is that the models were designed for use in isolation and not as part of a software federation. Spectral Sciences, Inc. will modify an existing framework developed in-house that supports modeling pipelines with high parallelization. In simple terms, we will add a timeline that controls simultaneous pipelines and provide a menu of three options for model upgrade (ranging from minimal to full participation) that model developers can implement for restart and playback participation. We will also research the complex topic of operating-system-based checkpoint and restart for possible introduction in the future. Approved for Public Release | 23-MDA-11635 (9 Nov 23)

Award Title: Low C-SWaP Long Wavelength Infrared Hyperspectral Imager Technology for Attritable Platforms
Research Area Keywords: attritable,  HSI,  image,  long-wavelength-infrared,  LWIR,  snapshot,  thermal, hyperspectral
Abstract: The ability to detect, identify, and warn against threats by state and non-state actors from airborne platforms in hostile airspace is a critical Air Force need to address Operational Imperative 3: Moving Target Engagement.? This need may be met using small, unmanned aerial system platforms (SUAS) carrying intelligence, surveillance, and reconnaissance (ISR) sensors.? Such sensors must be reliable and attritable, containing components with minimal maintenance and replacement cost.? Panchromatic, multispectral, and thermal cameras are insufficient to provide the necessary discrimination required to detect, identify, and defeat threats from existing and future adversaries.  Spectral Sciences Inc (SSI) is developing a LWIR HSI approach that promises the low C-SWaP equivalent to standard ISR thermal imaging cameras required for attritable SUAS operation.  Further, the proposed system has performance that rivals much larger HSI systems. An early prototype, SAFIRE (Superpixel Array For InfraREd) has been demonstrated. In this project, we propose new manufacturing and software processing approaches that reduce cost and enhance performance.  We introduce two innovations, one in hardware, to produce rugged, mechanically stable devices, and one in software, to correct and calibrate the data and produce informative detection results.

Award Title: Time-Accurate Hypersonic Modeling for Morphing Vehicles
RI Name: Regents of the University of Minnesota
Research Area Keywords: ablation,  computational fluid dynamics,  EO/IR signature,  morphing geometry,  RF signature,  time-accurate,  turbulence, Hypersonic
Abstract: Next-generation hypersonic vehicles must be designed for increased dynamics, control, and maneuverability to achieve enhanced tactical performance, speed, reach, and lethality. Morphing and adaptive structures, as well as active control surfaces, will be critical in achieving these goals. Changes in vehicle orientation and configuration along the vehicle flight path produce non-linear variations in freestream conditions, chemical kinetics, vibrational excitation, turbulence, ablation products, and gas-surface interactions, all of which affect the detectable emission spectra of the vehicle. Consequently, the emission intensity is also time-varying, making it particularly difficult to predict time-accurate electromagnetic (EM) signatures along the trajectory of a morphing vehicle. Designing high-performance morphing vehicles, therefore, requires highly accurate, tightly-coupled, physics-based modeling of these rate effects across a wide range of time scales. This R&D effort focuses on adding a Ātime-accurate toolkit to SSIÆs Hypersonic Scenes from Extreme Aerothermodynamics Systems, HySEAS. HySEAS is a robust framework of tools to couple high-fidelity, steady-state computational fluid dynamics and radiative transport models for end-to-end EM predictions over hypersonic trajectories. In Phase I, we implemented new capabilities for in-the-loop CFD mesh morphing and demonstrated capability for high-fidelity turbulence modeling. US3D was coupled with the novel TASC plugin for trajectory control and ablation modeling, and the EO/IR signature was predicted using SPPM-US3D. Phase I results for the end-to-end problem of a morphing hypersonic waverider over a full trajectory proved that our prototype toolkit can effectively capture time-dependent changes along the flight path. Substantial variations in the spectral intensity were predicted from changes to the vehicle shape in-flight, ablation effects, turbulence, altitude, and Mach number. In Phase II, we will develop new features and improve existing capabilities. We anticipate studying complex morphing geometries, and those which have existing validation/verification data available. We will expand our time-accurate toolkit to morph both fluid and solid CFD grids under ōactiveö morphing, such as a geometric change, and ōpassiveö shape-change, such as leading-edge erosion. Concurrently, our partners will focus on high-fidelity turbulence modeling with a particular focus on improving estimates for transition to turbulence. Since it is impractical to utilize these high-fidelity turbulence methods within a trajectory simulation loop, results will be used to identify pathways for optimizing RANS modeling within the HySEAS framework. The predicted flow-field will then be post-processed to obtain EO/IR and RF signatures for a morphing vehicle over a full trajectory.

Award Title: Automated Full Trajectory Aero-Thermo-Mechanical Simulation Coupling for Hypersonic Flight
Research Area Keywords: aerothermal modeling,  aerothermoelasticity,  computational fluid dynamics,  conjugate heat transfer,  finite element analysis,  thermostructural, Hypersonic flight
Abstract: Accurate prediction of the operating environment to design robust aero-thermo-mechanical loading capabilities of aeroshell materials will enable a better understanding of vehicle capabilities over Navy hypersonic flight trajectories.Ā Spectral Sciences, Inc., in collaboration with GoHypersonic Inc., has developed a detailed software plan that links high-fidelity aerothermal gas flow simulations using US3D and nonlinear thermomechanical finite element analysis (FEA) using ABAQUS for aero-thermo-mechanical simulations of hypersonic vehicle aeroshells along a flight trajectory. Phase I included development and demonstration of key components of this software, including automated mapping of thermal results between the two existing tools and development of an effective time discretization and coupling scheme. The initial prototype capability was demonstrated for select conditions. Phase II will develop the full prototype of the automated coupling software, which will include US3D plugin development, expanded capabilities for the existing Trajectory, Ablation, and Shape Change plugin, full workflow automation, and implementation of advanced aerodynamic and thermomechanical behaviors like transition to turbulence and thermostructural failure analysis. The prototype will then be tested against benchmark tests and with real Navy flight cases as part of an overall validation and verification effort. The proposed work will greatly simplify and streamline the simulation process, reducing the labor required to run a coupled aero-thermo-mechanical simulation over a full hypersonic flight trajectory.

Award Title: High-Altitude, Long-Endurance, Visible Through Extended SWIR Hyperspectral Imaging for Earth Sciences
Abstract: NASA has identified long-endurance, sub-orbital, stratospheric observations from High-Altitude Platform Systems (HAPS) as a key future capability for the Surface Biology and Geology (SBG) mission, and supporting the needs of commercial and government organizations for ecological, climate, earth resource and emergency management information.  A key source of data for SBG and other applications is hyperspectral imaging (HSI) over the Visible through Short Wave Infrared spectral region (VSWIR).  VSWIR HSI systems have been flown on aircraft below 50,000 ft and recently on low-Earth orbiting satellites. However, these platforms do not meet the combined wide-area coverage, spatial resolution, station keeping and long duration diurnal measurement requirements of SBG measurement campaigns.   A HAPS VSWIR HSI solution is therefore an important science mission objective for NASA. Spectral Sciences, Inc. (SSI) proposes an innovative combination of our rugged Terrestrial Hyperspectral Imaging Apparatus (THIA) pushbroom VSWIR HSI, the Sceye Inc. Stratospheric HAPS, and Hood Technologies gimbal technology to meet SBG sensing needs.  Our team proposes to fly THIA in a demonstration of HAPS capability to collect VSWIR HSI imagery from above 60,000 ft.  In Phase I, we will develop a concept of operations and preliminary mission plan for a THIA-HAPS flight.

Award Title: Moving Target Simulator (MTSim) Framework
Research Area Keywords: GMTI,  Machine Learning,  Sensors,  Synthetic Data,  Target Simulation, Scene Generation
Abstract: Ground Moving Target Indication (GMTI) from proliferated Low Earth Orbit (LEO) requires rapid detection and identification of moving targets from a sequence of imagery which contains platform induced motion, natural scene motion and target motion. Successful GMTI in such a complicated and cluttered environment requires new and innovative image processing techniques. Machine Learning (ML) may provide creative solutions in this domain but requires a large training set of images, which represent the full range of possible targets, backgrounds, and viewing scenarios.  Obtaining such training sets is not possible from measurement alone because operationally important scenarios may be absent or limited. Radiometrically accurate synthetic data can expand available training datasets to improve algorithm performance by providing large quantities of automatically labeled training data for rare scene events and mission critical non-ideal conditions. To address the need for the additional training imagery, Spectral Sciences, Inc. (SSI) proposes to develop the Moving Target Simulator (MTSim) framework for the simulation of time sequence synthetic imagery with embedded moving targets. MTSim combines state of the art 3D radiative transfer scene simulation with a sensor response module to enable the Air Force to produce accurate synthetic moving target imagery as measured by any on-orbit system.

The Data-Driven Predictive Sporadic-E Model (D2PSEM) will be an algorithm which provides a two-week to one-month forecast of the time-dependent probability of occurrence of sporadic-E layer (Es) of the ionosphere above a user-defined density threshold at arbitrary locations on the globe.  D2PSEM will operate on measured data to establish a set of correlations between relevant physical quantities in the temporal domain followed by a relationship between those correlations and the spatial domain.  Once developed, this function will be used to derive the key physical quantities of interest from a known set of correlative properties. The comprehensive correlation model will then be the kernel of a Data Assimilation Predictive Model that foreword propagates occurrences while updating the correlation coefficients based on the latest ionospheric data.

Award Title: Feature-aware Ablation and Reaction SImulator for Thermal Extremes (FARSITE)
RI Name: University of Colorado Boulder-Office of Contracts and Grants
Research Area Keywords: Ablation,  Carbon,  CFD,  Finite-Rate,  hypersonic,  Mesoscale,  TPS, TPS
Abstract: Hypersonic vehicles require thermal protection systems (TPS) for protection from high temperature, strongly shocked air. Carbon-based ablative TPS have received particular attention for Navy applications. Physics-based models of gas¢material interactions are needed for hypersonic vehicle design, since ground-based testing at flight-like conditions is limited and costly. In the proposed effort, Spectral Sciences, Inc., with academic partner University of Colorado, will develop innovative software to model carbon ablation with finite-rate chemistry and mesoscale material physics in computational fluid dynamics (CFD) workflows. The new software, called FARSITE (Feature-aware Ablation and Reaction SImulator for Thermal Extremes), will be a plugin for the US3D hypersonic CFD code. The Phase I effort will demonstrate feasibility of extensions to a state-of-the-art finite-rate ablation model that are informed by new experimental data and integrated into the CFD solver. Later-Phase efforts will implement additional, advanced models of molecular and mesoscale ablation processes, guided by comprehensive experimental validation campaigns on Navy-relevant materials such as 2D and 3D carbon¢carbon. FARSITE will meet critical Government and commercial needs to understand and manage gas¢material interactions in extreme thermal environments.

Award Title: Low C-SWaP Long Wavelength Infrared Hyperspectral Imager Technology for Attritable Platforms
Research Area Keywords: drone, HSI, Hyperspectral, image, Long-wavelength-infrared, Sensor, SUAS, thermal
Abstract: Small, unmanned aerial system platforms (SUAS) carrying intelligence, surveillance, and reconnaissance (ISR) sensors are becoming ubiquitous in military and intelligence operations.  Sensors may be operated frequently in hostile airspace, and therefore must be reliable and attritable, containing components with minimal maintenance and replacement cost.  Compact hyperspectral imaging (HSI) systems provide a new layer of information about material properties that aids detection and characterization of objects on the ground. Long-wave Infrared (LWIR) HSI combines the day-night operational capability of thermal imaging with the material characterization of HSI. However, the high cost, size, weight and power requirements (C-SWaP) of existing thermal HSI has prevented their adoption for attritable applications.  Spectral Sciences Inc (SSI) is developing a LWIR HSI approach that promises the low C-SWaP equivalent to standard ISR thermal imaging cameras required for attritable SUAS operation.  Further, the proposed system has performance that rivals much larger HSI systems. A prototype, SAFIRE (Superpixel Array For InfraREd), has been demonstrated. In this project, we propose new manufacturing and software processing approaches to SAFIRE.  We introduce two innovations, one in hardware, to produce rugged, mechanically stable devices, and one in software, to correct and calibrate the data and produce informative detection results.

Award Title: MCScene-Based Oceanic Radiative Transfer Testbed
Research Area Keywords: atmospheric science,  BACKGROUND,  Emission,  Radiative Transfer,  remote sensing,  Satellite,  Satellite Based Environmental Monitoring,  Spectral Analysis?, remote sensing
Abstract: There is an urgent need for a radiative transfer testbed that can accurately synthesize hyperspectral imagery to support the design of remote sensing instruments, development of new retrieval algorithms, and quantification of uncertainties.Ā Remote sensing of the ground, ocean, and atmosphere requires RT models that predict the signatures sensors measure given a known atmospheric state, including land topography, temperature, the oceanic state, sea constituent profiles, underwater surface bathymetry, and spectral spectral bidirectional reflectance distribution functions (BRDFs). The accurate modeling of 3D scene signatures which include cloud fields, structured backgrounds, weather, sea state, coastal influences, and/or embedded targets, remains a challenge due to the difficulty of solving of the RT equations in a cluttered environment as well as the difficulty of embedding measured or numerical weather prediction (NWP) environmental data. ĀSpectral Sciences, Inc. (SSI) proposes to leverage its existing high-fidelity fully 3D synthetic hyperspectral imagery tool (MCScene) specifically enhanced for oceanographic applications.Ā This effort will add spatial resolutions required to model the marine surface boundary layer, a fully resolved sea-state-dependent air-sea interface, and underwater propagation for calculation of water leaving radiances.

Award Title: Artificial Intelligence Tool for Chemical and Biological Plumes Detection
Research Area Keywords: aerosol discrimination, artificial intelligence, biological plumes, chemical plumes, DISSEMINATION, lidar, machine learning, plume classification
Abstract: Accurate and timely detection and classification of chemical and/or biological threat agents (CBA) used by an adversary in a battlefield environment is a crucial task for reconnaissance and force protection systems. A key technology component in CBA plume sensing systems is the light detection and ranging (LIDAR) system. In Phase I a preliminary AI algorithm based on the autoencoding technique was developed. Field LIDAR data from CBA releases were compressed, filtered for artifacts and used to train the algorithm. The algorithm was tested on a separate set of data. The algorithm discriminated between different release mechanisms. In Phase II we will develop end-to-end software based on the Phase I algorithms. The algorithms will be extended to include a classifier to automatically discriminate different types of aerosols and releases. A database of synthetic data produced from high fidelity dispersion models will be produced to supplement field data. This will enable train the algorithms to discriminate within wider variety of meteorological and terrain conditions, and aerosol interferents. AI algorithm training will be performed on high-performance computers and also transitioned to a GPU system to reduce training time. Once trained the algorithms will operate in real time.

Award Title: Hyperspectral Imaging Pyrometer (HIP)
Research Area Keywords: arcjet, Hypersonics, Hyperspectral, pyrometer, Sensor, Spectropyrometer, thermal imaging, TPS
Abstract: Characterization of thermal protection systems (TPS) for hypersonic vehicles rely on accurate knowledge of TPS temperature and emissivity.  It is important to characterize these materials both for development and optimization of our own vehicles and to ai

Award Title: Prediction of Hypersonic Missile Signatures with Advanced Chemistry and Radiation Models
Research Area Keywords: chemistry, EO/IR, hypersonic, Plasma, Radar, Radiation, signature, Turbulence
Abstract: Spectral Sciences, Inc. proposes to extend the existing suite of simulation tools, currently optimized for boost-glide configurations and apply the suite for powered hypersonic missile signature prediction. In Phase I, we have integrated physical models and demonstrated feasibility of the approach by applying the prototype code to select problems. The successful feasibility demonstration provides a clear path for preparing the suite for transition to the community through the Phase II effort, through further hardening of the software and validating the end-to-end prediction capabilities. An additional benefit of the design is that the interceptor may allow engagement of multiple threats simultaneously. Approved for Public Release | 22-MDA-11340 (16 Dec 22)

Award Title: Automated Full Trajectory Aero-Thermo-Mechanical Simulation Coupling for Hypersonic Flight
Research Area Keywords: aerothermal modeling, aerothermoelasticity, Computational Fluid Dynamics, Finite Element Analysis, Hypersonic flight
Abstract: Spectral Sciences, Inc., in collaboration with GoHypersonic Inc., proposes to develop a framework for linking high-fidelity aerothermal gas flow simulations and nonlinear thermomechanical finite element analysis (FEA) for hypersonic vehicles through streamlined and automated mapping of results between the models. This effort will include the selection of an appropriate material model for simulation of anisotropic heat conduction and radiation, persistent shape change associated with both thermal and mechanical loading, and potential material failure under such conditions. This effort will leverage SSI’s extensive experience with aerothermal modeling of hypersonic vehicles with US3D with extensions, including the Trajectory Ablation and Shape Change plugin. Phase I will develop a detailed plan for coupling the two codes and demonstrate the one-directional coupling between US3D and FEA with a representative 3D vehicle and material. Phase II will develop the full prototype mapping between the two simulations and further streamline the process through a user-friendly software module that automates  both simulations. This effort will greatly simplify the simulation process, thereby reducing the labor required to run an aero-thermo-mechanical simulation over a full hypersonic flight trajectory.

Award Title: Physics-Informed Machine Learning for Hypersonic Turbulence
Research Area Keywords: artificial intelligence, computational fluid dynamics (CFD), Deep Learning, Hypersonics, machine learning, Neural networks, Turbulence
Abstract: Turbulence modeling for compressible flow is essential for accurate modeling of heat transfer, skin friction, and pressure surrounding hypersonic vehicles to correctly predict vehicle survivability. High fidelity computational fluid dynamics (CFD) approaches are too computationally demanding and require faster, workhorse methods such as Reynolds Averaged Navier-Stokes (RANS) simulations for computation of flight trajectories and dynamics. However, current RANS turbulence closure models cannot accurately characterize hypersonic flowfield features. Physics-informed machine learning (PIML) methods provide an avenue for creation of turbulence models. PIML methods can use experimental and high-fidelity simulation data to learn models constrained to the physical governing equations, resulting in a more general and extendible turbulence model. In this STTR effort, Spectral Sciences, Inc. and Virginia Tech will develop an innovative PIML turbulence closure model leveraging recent advances in ensemble learning to compute accurate flowfield gradients for efficient training of the neural network-based approach. Phase I will develop a protype software tool and demonstrate the approach on a system of interest, with the aim of showing significant improvement for important flow quantities. Phase II will extend the approach to a wider range of flow conditions and integrate it into a software toolkit for hypersonic flow.

Award Title: Mass Spectral Sensor for Post-intercept Assessment
Research Area Keywords: Debris, homeland defense, In-situ sensor, Interceptor, mass spectrometry, Missile, post-intercept assessment
Abstract: Spectral Sciences in collaboration with Busek, Corvid Technologies, and Control Vision, Inc. proposes a MAss Spectral Sensor for Post-Intercept Assessment (MASSPIA). Phase I involves development and demonstration of the concept using state-of-the-art post-intercept debris cloud modeling tools, and a preliminary design of the mass spectral sensor. Approved for Public Release | 22-MDA-11215 (27 Jul 22)

Award Title: High Sensitivity Tracking for Event-Based LEO Moving Target Indication
Research Area Keywords: Detection and Tracking, EBS camera, EBS modeling, electro-optical performance modeling, Event-Based Sensing (EBS), ground moving target indication (GMTI), Moving Target Indication (MTI), scene simulation
Abstract: The Air Force is exploring the applicability of event-based sensing (EBS) for intelligence, surveillance, and reconnaissance from low earth orbit. These sensors would be deployed in a proliferated, hybrid satellite architecture for the detection of moving

Award Title: Utilizing ML Algorithms to Track and Identify UAS Threats
Abstract: Unmanned aerial systems (UASs) present a growing threat in the US defense arena as both low-cost intelligence gathering and payload delivery capabilities for adversaries, posing security risks to both military and civilian assets. Detecting, tracking, and identifying UAS threats has been accomplished with conventional imaging and radar techniques, but each technology has limitations. Passive imaging systems have excellent spatial resolution; however, their system complexity and cost are driven by day/night operation requirements that need different sensor systems for solar reflection and/or thermal emission as the primary detection method. Radar based solutions have broad spatial detection coverage but poor resolution for precise target location and identification. Active lidar has the potential to fill the gap where other methodologies fall short by providing day/night operation at high spatial resolution. Many lidar systems exist with performance capabilities dependent on design application. For example, the automotive industry has been using low-SWAP lidar for spatial awareness with machine learning (ML). ML is an excellent method for object identification but relies heavily on man-in-the-loop model training. Variation in real-world target scenes have a strong influence on the performance of these algorithms. Different scenarios present different types of in-scene clutter or detection noise that can negatively impact the reliability of models. It is unclear as to which lidar system would perform best when integrated into a counter UAS system with ML automated target identification. Spectral Sciences Inc.’s (SSI) is a leader in electro-optical performance analysis for remote sensing, measurement simulation, and target detection strategies. In Phase I, we propose design trade studies on different lidar system specifications that will maximize UAS detection range, accuracy, and sensitivity, while identifying a feasible low-SWAP hardware configuration coupled with ML algorithms for a SOCOM counter UAS (C-UAS) system. We will reduce the complexity of the training data for ML algorithms and pre-process measured lidar data to improve ML identification accuracy and precision by utilizing our advanced clutter suppression signal processing techniques. SSI's state of the art radiation transport modeling capabilities will address the well-established need for synthetic data during ML training reducing the risk and time for costly measurements and data labeling while increasing the variety of real-world measurement scenarios needed for a reliable ML based C-UAS system. In Phase II, these methods will be combined to develop a lidar system with automated ML acquisition of UAS profiles, locations, and threat assessments.

Award Title: Enhanced Modeling and Simulations of Hypersonics
Research Area Keywords: chemistry, EO/IR, hypersonic, Optical, Plasma, Radar, Radiation, signature
Abstract: The conditions encountered by hypersonic vehicles are complex due to the non-linear, strong interaction between the flow field environment and vehicle.  Successful modeling requires multi-physics capabilities that can properly take into account high-temperature gas dynamics, which includes excitation of internal energy modes, finite-rate gas chemistry, turbulent flow, and complex plasma physics, as well as the chemical and mechanical response of the vehicle heat shield surface, which includes finite-rate surface chemistry, chemical decomposition, heat conduction, and surface vibration.  Spectral Sciences, Inc. proposes to enhance the existing suite of simulation tools.  In Phase I, we will select key algorithmic improvements to enhance the prediction of plasma formation in the coupled environment to demonstrate a practical and accurate end-to-end simulation capability for both radar and optical signatures.  Furthermore, the feasibility demonstration will provide a clear path for further enhancement of the tool during Phase II.  The proposed approach is based on several innovative capabilities in our simulation capabilities including: a novel way to include critical chemical mechanisms that are important to observables, prediction of the hypersonic environment surrounding 3D vehicle shapes that include conjugate heating effects, and unique post-processing capabilities to predict both radar and optical observables from the complex environment.

Award Title: Prediction of Hypersonic Cruise Missile Signatures with Advanced Chemistry and Radiation Modules
Research Area Keywords: chemistry, hypersonic, Plasma, Radar, Radiation, signature, Turbulence
Abstract: Spectral Sciences, Inc. proposes to extend the existing suite of simulation tools, currently optimized for boost-glide configurations, and apply the suite for hypersonic cruise missile signature prediction. In Phase I, we will integrate missing physical models and demonstrate feasibility of the approach by applying the prototype code to select problems. The feasibility demonstration will provide a clear path for further hardening and enhancements of the suite during Phase II. The proposed approach is based on several innovative capabilities, which enable end-to-end prediction of the hypersonic environment and resulting signature across relevant trajectories for 3D vehicle shapes using high-fidelity models. Approved for Public Release | 21-MDA-11013 (19 Nov 21)

Award Title: Moving Target Simulator (MTSim) Framework
Research Area Keywords: AMTI, GMTI, machine learning, Scene generation, Sensors, Synthetic Data, target simulation
Abstract: Airborne Moving Target Indication (AMTI) from proliferated Low Earth Orbit (LEO) requires rapid detection and identification of moving targets from a sequence of imagery which contains platform induced motion, natural scene motion and target motion. Successful AMTI in such a complicated and cluttered environment requires new and innovative image processing techniques. Machine Learning (ML) may provide creative solutions in this domain but requires a large training set of images, which represent the full range of possible targets, backgrounds, and viewing scenarios.  Obtaining such training sets is not possible from measurement alone because operationally important scenarios may be absent or limited. Radiometrically accurate synthetic data can expand available training datasets to improve algorithm performance by providing large quantities of automatically labeled training data for rare scene events and mission critical non-ideal conditions. To address the need for the additional training imagery, Spectral Sciences, Inc. (SSI) proposes to develop the Moving Target Simulator (MTSim) framework for the simulation of time sequence synthetic imagery with embedded moving targets. MTSim combines state of the art 3D radiative transfer scene simulation with a sensor response module to enable the Air Force to produce accurate synthetic moving target imagery as measured by any on-orbit system.

Award Title: Predictive Model for Sensor Effects from Aberrating Vehicle Environments With Spectra (SEAViEWS)
Research Area Keywords: Aero-optics, Computational Fluid Dynamics, hypersonic, non-local thermodynamic equilibrium, Radiative Transfer, Sensors, Turbulence
Abstract: The complex interplay between turbulent high-speed aerodynamics and thermo-chemical nonequilibrium in the hypersonic regime has a significant impact on optical sensors. This includes unsteady distortions like image blur from turbulence, steady distortions like image tilts from shock waves, and  signal-to-noise-ratio degradation from background radiation/emissions. Currently, no stand-alone tool exists that predicts both the highly complex thermo-chemical nonequilibrium processes and the high-speed aerodynamic aberrating mechanisms. To fill this gap, Spectral Sciences, Inc. (SSI) in conjunction with Professors Iain Boyd and Graham Candler propose an innovative simulation tool, SEAViEWS, which leverages high-fidelity models of hypersonic flow, radiative transport through non-equilibrium gases, and wavefront propagation. Our Phase I effort will demonstrate the feasibility of the tool by focusing on EO/IR sensors viewing through hypersonic turbulent boundary layers. This will lay the foundation to scale-up SEAViEWS by ensuring the relevant turbulent scales are predicted, applicable real gas chemistry effects are accounted for, and appropriate wavefront propagation mechanisms are modeled. The final product will improve the sensor design process and aid in developing mitigation technologies for optical distortions. With expertise in hypersonic thermo-chemical nonequilibrium, turbulence, and high-fidelity modeling, our team is ideally suited to produce a successful SEAViEWS prototype and transition it to Navy applications.

Award Title: Comprehensive Optical Post-Processing tool (COPPT)
Research Area Keywords: background clutter, Debris, Electro-optical, Hydrocode, hydrostructural code, Infrared, Missile Defense, post-intercept assessment, remote sensing, spectral data
Abstract: Spectral Sciences, Inc. (SSI), proposes extensive upgrades, benchmarking, and post-intercept assessment (PIA) prototype integration of the Comprehensive Optical Post-Processing Tool (COPPT). SSI will lead a Team of experts including Corvid Technologies, SciTec, Sandia National Laboratory, and Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory. COPPT is an EO/IR scene generation code that post-processes outputs from first-principles hydrocodes and hydrostructural models (FPCs). In the proposed Phase II effort, COPPT will be upgraded for compatibility with the latest versions of CTH and ALE3D hydrocodes, and interfaces will be developed for the hydrostructural codes, Zapotec and Paradyn. Long duration post-intercept simulations will then be possible that combine micro-debris diffuse and high contrast discrete debris signatures computed from source terms generated by a variety of FPCs. The COPPT spectral and sensor models will also be upgraded for more comprehensive EO/IR scene generation and sensor design testing. The upgraded COPPT will be tested and benchmarked against thermal flash radiometric data using the same hypervelocity impact cases modeled by FPC models from Corvid (Velodyne) and the Department of Energy. The results of the benchmarking will be incorporated in a Basis-of-Confidence document. The upgraded COPPT will be integrated in PIA architecture prototypes. Approved for Public Release | 22-MDA-11102 (22 Mar 22)

Award Title: Time-Accurate Modeling for Hypersonic Morphing Vehicles
RI Name: Regents of the University of Minnesota
Research Area Keywords: ablation, Computational Fluid Dynamics, EO/IR signature, Fluid-Structure Interactions, hypersonic, morphing geometry, RF signature, Turbulence
Abstract: To achieve enhanced tactical performance, speed, reach, and lethality, next-generation hypersonic vehicles must be designed for increased dynamics, control, and maneuverability. Morphing and adaptive structures, and active control surfaces will be critical in achieving these goals. However, the time-varying flow-field brought about by changes in vehicle orientation and configuration make such designs particularly difficult. The variations include non-linear changes in atmospheric conditions, chemical kinetics, vibrational excitation, turbulence, ablation products, and gas-surface interactions. The resultant changes in emissions intensity and spectral band means that mitigating strong electromagnetic signatures becomes a critical design requirement for stealth. Computational fluid dynamics (CFD) remains the most promising design tool for predicting these complex flow-fields, but requires robust techniques for tightly coupling the fluid, thermal, kinetic, and structural solutions for a single time-varying system. The goal of this R&D is to develop an innovative, high-fidelity toolkit capable of predicting time-accurate electromagnetic signatures by modeling the tightly-coupled, time-varying flowfield phenomenon occurring across a range of flight conditions, including rapid maneuvers and potential morphological changes. The Phase I methodology will focus on laying the fundamental theoretical groundwork by demonstrating time-accurate EO/IR signature prediction along a trajectory with small changes in vehicle geometry. We will utilize US3D to predict the chemical kinetics and turbulence along the full flight path, accounting for changes in vehicle geometry using in-the-loop CFD grid deformations. The resulting flow-field will be used to predict EO/IR signatures. Phase I will also include running Large Eddy Simulations for a subset of the flow-field. Finally, we will develop a comprehensive physics-based road-map for software expansion to TRL 5. Work in Phase II and beyond will involve expanding the Phase I prototype to include in-the-loop grid morphing, ablation, fluid-structure interactions, and radar signature prediction. We will also integrate the high-fidelity LES approaches demonstrated in Phase I into the toolkit framework.

Award Title: Software for First Principles-Based Predictions of the Survivability of EO/IR Sensor Materials Exposed to the Radiation Environment of Space
Research Area Keywords: crystal defect, density functional theory, EO/IR sensor, Material Science, microelectronic survivability, Molecular Dynamics, radiation damage
Abstract: The micro- and opto-electronic components, such as electro-optic/infrared (EO/IR) sensors, can be damaged due to exposure to the harsh radiation environment of space. Computational predictions of radiation-induced damage to the materials that give these components their functionality show promise in helping to determine how to harden these devices. In the proposed work, we will investigate how to model the radiation-induced damage in EO/IR sensor materials with atomic resolution over the multiple timescales involved. Monte Carlo (MC) simulations will be employed to describe the radiation-material interaction and produce initial conditions for molecular dynamics (MD) studies. MD will then be used to simulate the damage following irradiation. We will investigate other computational techniques for simulating long-term evolution of the damage to obtain a steady state description. The survivability of the material will be assessed based on first principles electronic structure calculations. Density functional theory (DFT) will be employed to predict and compare the electronic properties of both the damaged and undamaged material to determine if the functionality of the material was compromised. The Phase I will include demonstration of the MC, MD, and DFT studies, and development of a plan for prototype software development in Phase II. Approved for Public Release | 22-MDA-11215 (27 Jul 22)

Award Title: Rare Target and Anomaly Detection in Overhead Imagery using Spatial Feature Vectors
Research Area Keywords: aerosol discrimination, artificial intelligence, Autonomy, biological plumes, chemical plumes, dissemination., lidar, light-induced detection and ranging, machine learning, mass transport and dispersion, Pattern recognition, plume classification, plume morphology, plume recognition
Abstract: Accurate and timely detection and classification of chemical and/or biological threat agents (CBA) used by an adversary in a battlefield environment is a crucial task for reconnaissance and force protection systems. A key technology component in CBA plume sensing systems is the light detection and ranging (LIDAR) system. The LIDAR laser propagates through and backscatters off of aerosol plume droplets, producing a characteristic return related to the CBA dissemination mechanism. In the operational space of a high-intensity conflict, airborne particulates generated by vehicle movement and weapons effects create a cluttered background that reduces the effectiveness of LIDAR detection. There is a need for advanced automated tools that can separate cases of deliberate dissemination from more common kinetic events ubiquitous to the battlefield environment. Toward this objective, Spectral Sciences, Inc. (SSI) proposes to develop artificial intelligence algorithms built on an artificial intelligence (AI) framework to rapidly classify LIDAR returns as CBA or non-CBA. The proposed solution will utilize the localization of dissemination events to adopt a transfer learning approach to reduce a demand on training data. The Phase I work will prototype the basic approach and demonstrate the feasibility of achieving these goals.

Award Title: Hypersonic Wake Detection with High Enthalpy Capabilities
Research Area Keywords: chemiluminescence, High Enthalpy, hypersonic, laminar-turbulent transition, Reentry Vehicles, Wake Turbulence
Abstract: The distribution of observable markers in hypersonic wakes is the result of a complex interaction of body shape, chemical kinetics, and laminar-turbulent transition mechanisms.  To understand the complex gas structure, new measurement techniques are required to improve the understanding of the laminar-to-turbulence transition, separation dynamics just behind the body, and turbulence statistics and structure in the wake, particularly for the species that drive the gas emission.  To address this need, Spectral Sciences, Inc. proposes to integrate multiple line-of-sight spectral measurements combined with advanced algorithms to interrogate small volume locations of the flow.  SSI will leverage extensive experience in development of spectral sensors to interrogate high-temperature environments with the capability to apply high-fidelity description of hypersonic shock layer and wake signature prediction capabilities.  This spectral information allows users to understand the gas composition, temperature, and velocity of point locations within the flow field of interest. In Phase I, the feasibility of the algorithm will be demonstrated using existing data and high-fidelity measurements.  In Phase II, the algorithms will be integrated with hardware and applied in available tests to measure high enthalpy gas flows around relevant geometries.

Award Title: OPIR Radiometric Calibration
Research Area Keywords: bidirectional reflectance distribution function, CAL/VAL, Calibration, Cross-calibration, OBAC, OPIR, SBIRS, vicarious calibration
Abstract: The Air Force is exploring alternative uses for Overhead Persistent Infrared Missile Warning (OPIR-MW) systems, which require improved on-orbit sensor radiometric calibrations. These systems are heavily tasked by their primary mission, so calibration must be accomplished without additional tasking or ground support. Spectral Sciences, Inc. proposes an innovative calibration scheme based on cross calibration of the OPIR data stream with contemporaneous measurements from the geostationary operational environmental satellites and Japanese Himawari weather imaging satellites, which collectively provide continuous, high quality radiometrically-calibrated imagery with exceptional Earth coverage. In Phase I, we employed novel real-time bidirectional ground reflectance models with optimized spectral band fitting to generate accurate calibration constants for OPIR spectral bands. These proposed innovations will simplify using advanced simulations, improve accuracy by accounting for variable reflectance, and work for all OPIR wavebands, including those with strong atmospheric absorption features. The Phase I proof-of-concept clearly demonstrated this procedure for imaging systems with OPIR equivalent bands. In Phase II, we will develop the REmote Sensing Precise ON-orbit SEnsor calibration (RESPONSE) software toolkit to provide general capability for satellite sensor calibration and conduct comprehensive testing and analysis focused on using live data sources in an operational-like environment.

Award Title: Enhanced Modeling and Simulation of Hypersonics
Research Area Keywords: chemistry, EO/IR, hypersonic, Optical, Plasma, Radar, Radiation, signature
Abstract: The conditions encountered by hypersonic vehicles are complex due to the non-linear, strong interaction between the flow field environment and vehicle.  Successful modeling requires multi-physics capabilities that can properly take into account high-temperature gas dynamics, which includes excitation of internal energy modes, finite-rate gas chemistry, turbulent flow, and complex plasma physics, as well as the chemical and mechanical response of the vehicle heat shield surface, which includes finite-rate surface chemistry, chemical decomposition, heat conduction, and surface vibration.  Spectral Sciences, Inc. proposes to enhance the existing suite of simulation tools.  In Phase I, we will select key algorithmic improvements to enhance the prediction of plasma formation in the coupled environment to demonstrate a practical and accurate end-to-end simulation capability for both radar and optical signatures.  Furthermore, the feasibility demonstration will provide a clear path for further enhancement of the tool during Phase II.  The proposed approach is based on several innovative capabilities in our simulation capabilities including: a novel way to include critical chemical mechanisms that are importance to observables, prediction of the hypersonic environment surrounding 3D vehicle shapes that include conjugate heating effects, and unique post-processing capabilities to predict both radar and optical observables from the complex environment.

Award Title: Machine Learning Algorithm for Calculating Wavefronts from a Single Coherent Beam Return
Research Area Keywords: ‘artificial intelligence’, ‘coherent imaging’, ‘digital holography’, ‘machine learning’, ‘wavefront sensing’
Abstract: The accuracy of future military electro-optical (EO) sensors for intelligence, reconnaissance, and surveillance (ISR), laser beam control, seeker systems and space surveillance, that operate along turbulent atmospheric lines-of-sight, can be enhanced using coherent wavefront sensing. The intervening atmospheric turbulence and vehicle wake distort the sensor observation, reducing target contrast and image resolution. A promising approach to real-time detection is single-shot digital holography (SSDH), in which a laser beam illuminates the target, and the return is processed to produce a reconstruction of the wavefront and a turbulence corrected image. The processing includes optical interference with a reference laser, retrieving the wavefront, and applying the wavefront to the return image to mitigate both atmospheric and laser speckle effects. It uses signal estimation methods to remove turbulence and speckle effects and correct the image. One of the current limitations to advancement of SSDH is the computational burden of the current physics-based Bayesian estimation approach to processing, which would require that the vehicle carry a massive on-board computer capability to produce real-time results. Spectral Sciences, Inc., in collaboration with researchers at New Mexico State University, propose to replace current computational approaches with a machine learning algorithm to speed up the processing by orders of magnitude. Our algorithm training will be informed by modeling of the sensor, vehicle wake, atmosphere, and potential targets. In Phase I, we will construct the algorithm, test it to assess its speed and performance with modeled inputs, and consider scalability. In Phase II, we will further develop the algorithm, demonstrate its performance in an optical breadboard SSDH system, and define a computational system for on-board operations.

Award Title: System for Day/Night Detection of UAVs Using Spectral MWIR/LWIR Imaging
Research Area Keywords: counter-unmanned aerial vehicle (C-UAV), Day/Night, detection, Hyperspectral, identification, image, imaging, Infrared, Longwave-infrared, LWIR, Midwave-Infrared, Multi-spectral, Multispectral, MWIR, nighttime, UAV, UNMANNED AERIAL VEHICLES
Abstract: Unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) carrying surveillance equipment, targeting systems and even explosives and bombs, are a growing threat to US armed forces and government agencies.   This has in turn produced a need for systems to detect UAVs around naval ships, military bases, government and public facilities and national infrastructure.  Spectral imagers, operating in the Midwave Infrared (MWIR) and Longwave Infrared (LWIR) atmospheric windows, have significant potential to provide day/night detection and classification of UAVs under a variety of terrain and sky conditions and discrimination from potential interfering objects like birds.  For such complex environments, it is imperative that the overall design of the sensor, and especially the selection of spectral windows covered, and spatial, spectral, and temporal resolution, be paired with specialized algorithms for detection, tracking and classification in highly cluttered conditions.  Spectral Sciences, Inc. (SSI) has significant experience with both spectral imaging system design and development, and spectral detection algorithms.  For this SBIR effort, we propose to develop a set of algorithms that will be used to detect UAVs using their spatial, spectral and temporal characteristics, together with sensor hardware and software specifications for optimal UAV detection.   In Phase I, we will collect high resolution spectral imagery from a number of UAVs, both stationary and in flight, against clear sky and cluttered backgrounds, to clarify the challenges to spectral detection.  Beginning from state-of-the-art spectral anomaly detection and material classification algorithms, our detection and classification algorithms will address specific issues of UAV detection, including atmospheric effects, frame to frame motion, day/night detection, and clutter suppression.

Award Title: An Architecture for Calculating Transient High-Altitude Radiant Energy from Agile Targets
Research Area Keywords: chemistry high altitude plumes, continuum fluid dy, continuum fluid dynamics (CFD), direct simulation Monte Carlo (DSMC), Electro-Optical Signatures, high altitude plumes, Maneuvering, Modeling, threat missile systems
Abstract: The objective of this proposed SBIR effort is to develop improved signature modeling capabilities for new and emerging threat systems.  The proposed software will combine the next-generation simulation tools needed to calculate accurate plume flowfields and signatures for dim, maneuvering targets into a single software platform to facilitate the calculation of spectral signatures of maneuvering threat missile systems over extended trajectories.  When complete, this new software capability will enable analysts to go from a simple description of the threat system to an accurate prediction of an extended EO plume signature with only minimal knowledge of the individual simulation tools.

Award Title: Hypersonic Optical Signature Toolkit for Transient Effects
Research Area Keywords: Chuff, Debris, hypersonic, Infrared, Modeling, Optical Signatures, transient effects, Vehicles
Abstract: Spectral Sciences, Inc., in collaboration with Sierra Engineering and Software, Inc., proposes to develop a predictive, data-validated software tool, which we have dubbed the Hypersonics Optical Signature Toolkit (HOST), for synthetically generating spatially and temporally-dependent EO signatures of maneuvering hypersonic configurations containing solid rocket boost and directional motors.  This effort will not only leverage many of SSI’s current high-fidelity wake and SRM debris modeling tools, it will interface these up-until-now disparate capabilities into a single, comprehensive signature tool for complex hypersonic systems.

Award Title: Target Identification Using Machine Learning-Based Fusion of Hyperspectral and 3D Imagery
Research Area Keywords: Data Fusion, HSI, hyperspectral imaging, lidar, Neural network, target detection
Abstract: Target detection and identification in the Hyperspectral Imaging (HSI) domain relies on the uniqueness of spectral signatures, whereas classification in the spatial domain relies on morphological attributes of the target. Therefore, a detection and classification approach that fuses information from the spectral and spatial domains has the potential to considerably increase target detection confidence and reduce false positives. In Phase I, we will prototype a new spectral-spatial fusion processing chain that represents a significant advance over existing methods, which were developed mainly for surface and terrain classification. Target Bidirectional Reflectance Distribution Function (BDRF) and shadow effects are accounted for using advanced HSI target detection algorithms to produce provisional detection scores. Potential target-containing regions of interest (ROIs) are further identified using 3-D shape information contained in Digital Surface Models. Target detections and identifications are made with a machine learning algorithm followed by fusion with HSI scores. The Phase I work, conducted in collaboration with the RIT Center for Imaging Science, will provide a proof-of-principle for the development of deliverable software in Phase II.

Award Title: Modernized Radiative Transfer Software for Missile and Hypersonic Threat Signatures
Research Area Keywords: atmosphere, hypersonic, Missile, Plume, radiance, signature, Spectrum, wake
Abstract: Spectral Sciences, Inc. (SSI) proposes to combine two of its established radiative transfer codes, MODTRAN® and Fast Line-of-sight Absorption and Molecular Emission Spectra (FLAMES) into one extensible radiative transfer and scene generation tool that greatly simplifies the process of computing sophisticated radiation signatures in the infra-red (IR) band for sensors at any distance from a source of radiance.  This new product is dubbed Hyperspectral Imagery for Plume and Wake Radiation (HIPWR).
